description: Hexa is a multi-paradigm programming language built around the perfect number n=6. It features ownership semantics, algebraic effects, green-thread concurrency, hygienic macros, compile-time evaluation, and a formal proof system. The interpreter is written in Rust.

io.name: print
io.description: Print arguments separated by spaces, no trailing newline

io.name: println
io.description: Print arguments separated by spaces with trailing newline

io.name: format
io.description: Format string with {} placeholders replaced by args

number_theory.name: sopfr
number_theory.description: Sum of prime factors with repetition

string.name: len
string.description: Length of string, array, or map

collections.name: keys
collections.description: Get all keys of a map

collections.name: values
collections.description: Get all values of a map

collections.name: has_key
collections.description: Check if map contains a key

collections.name: Set
collections.description: Create a new Set from values

conversion.name: type_of
conversion.description: Get type name of a value

json.name: json_parse
json.description: Parse JSON string into Hexa value

json.name: json_stringify
json.description: Serialize Hexa value to JSON string

http.name: http_get
http.description: HTTP GET request, returns response body

http.name: http_post
http.description: HTTP POST request, returns response body

char.name: is_digit
char.description: Check if character is a digit

concurrency.name: join
concurrency.description: Wait for a spawned future to complete

memory.name: mem_region
memory.description: Stats for a specific memory region

grammar_patterns.pure_function: pure fn name(param: Type) -> RetType { body }
grammar_patterns.async_function: async fn name(param: Type) -> RetType { body }
grammar_patterns.comptime_function: comptime fn name(param: Type) -> RetType { body }
grammar_patterns.generic_function: fn name<T: Bound>(param: T) -> T where T: Trait { body }
grammar_patterns.impl: impl Type { fn method(self) -> Type { body } }
grammar_patterns.impl_trait: impl Trait for Type { fn method(self) -> Type { body } }
grammar_patterns.match: match expr { Pattern1 -> result1, Pattern2(val) -> result2, _ -> default }
grammar_patterns.match_with_guard: match expr { pattern if guard -> result }
grammar_patterns.select: select { rx1.recv() as val => { body }, timeout(1000) => { body } }
grammar_patterns.effect_decl: effect Name { fn operation(param: Type) -> RetType }
grammar_patterns.theorem: theorem name { forall x: type, cond => conclusion }
grammar_patterns.visibility: pub fn / pub struct (levels: private, mod, crate, pub)

string.name: join
string.description: Join array elements with this string as separator

string.name: index_of
string.description: Find index of substring (-1 if not found)

string.name: pad_left
string.description: Pad string on left to given width

string.name: pad_right
string.description: Pad string on right to given width

array.name: push
array.description: Return new array with item appended

array.name: filter
array.description: Keep elements where function returns true

set.name: remove
set.description: Remove item, returns whether it was present

option.name: unwrap
option.description: Extract value or panic on None

result.name: unwrap
result.description: Extract Ok value or panic on Err

result.name: unwrap_or
result.description: Extract Ok value or return default

receiver.name: try_recv
receiver.description: Non-blocking receive, returns Some(val) or None

examples.code: println("Hello, Hexa!")

let x = 42
let mut y = 3.14
let name: string = "hexa"
const MAX = 100
static counter = 0

let s = "Hello, World!"
println(s.len())
println(s.to_upper())
println(s.split(", "))
println(s.contains("World"))
println(s.replace("World", "Hexa"))

let config = #{"host": "localhost", "port": 8080}
println(config["host"])
println(config.keys())
println(config.len())

let x = own 42
let y = move x
// x is now moved, accessing it would error
println(y)
drop y

examples.description: Trait and impl for dynamic dispatch

notes.semicolons: Optional. Newlines serve as statement terminators.
notes.immutability: Variables are immutable by default. Use 'let mut' for mutable variables.
notes.arrays_immutable: Array methods like push(), sort(), reverse() return new arrays rather than mutating in place.
notes.index_access: Use [index] for arrays and [key] for maps.
notes.match_arrow: Match arms use -> (not =>). Fat arrow => is used in effect handlers.
notes.proof_blocks: proof blocks run assertions and report pass/fail. They are like test blocks.
notes.comptime_restrictions: comptime blocks cannot perform I/O (print, file ops, etc.).
notes.channel_returns_array: channel() returns a 2-element array [sender, receiver], not a tuple.
notes.spawn_returns_future: spawn { ... } returns a Future that can be checked with .is_ready() or awaited with join().
